topics.meaning: Science is an organised way of studying and understanding the natural world by observing, questioning and reasoning.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Science is much more than a set of facts in a textbook; it is a way of exploring and understanding the world around us. It studies everything in nature, living things, materials, the Earth, the sky and the forces that act on them. Science grows out of human curiosity, our wish to know how and why things happen. By observing carefully, asking questions, testing ideas and reasoning logically, science builds reliable knowledge about the world. Science is also useful, as it helps us solve problems, invent new things and improve our lives.
topics.easyExplanation: Science is a careful way of understanding the natural world by observing, questioning and reasoning. It grows from our curiosity about how and why things happen.

topics.meaning: The scientific method is a step-by-step process of observing, questioning, guessing, testing and concluding.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Scientists follow a careful, step-by-step process called the scientific method to find answers. It begins with observing something and asking a question about it. Next, a possible answer, called a hypothesis, is guessed. This hypothesis is then tested through experiments or further observations. The results are analysed, and a conclusion is drawn about whether the hypothesis was correct. If it was not, a new hypothesis is made and tested again. This orderly process makes scientific knowledge reliable and is used by scientists all over the world.
topics.easyExplanation: The scientific method is a step-by-step way to find answers: observe, question, guess (hypothesis), test, analyse, and conclude.

topics.meaning: Observation is carefully noticing things, and good questions come from observing the world closely.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The first step in science is observation, carefully noticing things using our senses and sometimes instruments. Good observation means paying close attention to details, like the colour, shape, sound or behaviour of things. Careful observation naturally leads us to ask questions: Why does this happen? How does it work? What would happen if...? Asking good questions is at the heart of science, because every discovery begins with a question. The more keenly we observe, the better the questions we can ask, and the more we can learn.
topics.easyExplanation: Observation means noticing things carefully with our senses. Good observation leads to good questions, which are the start of all science.

topics.meaning: An experiment is a fair test carried out to check whether a hypothesis is correct.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An experiment is a planned test that scientists do to check whether their hypothesis is true. In an experiment, we change one thing and observe what happens, while keeping other conditions the same so the test is fair. We make careful measurements and record what we observe. Repeating an experiment and getting the same result makes us more confident in the answer. Experiments must also be done safely, following rules and being careful with materials and equipment. Experiments turn guesses into tested knowledge.

topics.meaning: After an experiment, we analyse the results and draw a conclusion about whether the hypothesis was correct.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Once an experiment is done and the observations are recorded, scientists analyse the results, that is, they study them carefully to see what they mean. They look for patterns and compare what happened with what they expected. From this analysis, they draw a conclusion: was the hypothesis correct or not? If the results support the hypothesis, it becomes more trustworthy; if they do not, the hypothesis is changed and tested again. Drawing honest conclusions from evidence, even when they are surprising, is an important part of being a scientist.

topics.meaning: We use scientific thinking in many everyday activities, often without realising it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Science is not only found in laboratories; it is part of our daily lives. Whenever we ask 'how' or 'why' and try to find out, we are doing science. A cook wondering why the dal spilled, an electrician finding why a bulb won't glow, or a farmer choosing the best time to sow seeds are all thinking scientifically. Science is also behind the things we use every day, like soap, medicines, vehicles, phones and electricity. Recognising the science in everyday life helps us become curious, careful observers and better problem-solvers.

topics.meaning: Scientific knowledge keeps changing and improving as new evidence and ideas are found.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Science is described as 'ever-evolving' because our understanding of the world keeps growing and changing. As scientists make new observations and experiments, they gather new evidence that can support, improve or even replace older ideas. Many things we know today were discovered by building on the work of earlier scientists, and our knowledge will keep improving in future. This shows that science is open to change and correction; a scientific idea is accepted because of evidence, and can be revised if better evidence is found. This openness is a great strength of science.

topics.meaning: Science is rarely done alone; scientists across the world work together to advance knowledge.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although we often think of a scientist working alone, science is usually done by people working together. Scientists across the world share their observations, ideas and results with one another, so that others can check and build on their work. Working in teams and sharing knowledge helps science progress faster and makes the results more reliable, because many people test the same ideas. Anyone who observes carefully, asks questions and reasons logically is thinking like a scientist, so we can all take part in science, especially by working and learning together.

chapters.chapter: Exploring Substances: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral

topics.meaning: Acidic substances are substances that are sour to taste and turn blue litmus red.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many substances around us are acidic. Acidic substances usually have a sour taste, like lemon juice, vinegar, tamarind and curd. They contain acids. We should never taste unknown substances to test them, because many acids are harmful and can burn; instead we use indicators. Acids are found in nature, for example citric acid in lemons and oranges, and acetic acid in vinegar, and also in our stomach, which contains an acid that helps digest food. Acidic substances turn blue litmus paper red, which is a safe way to identify them.

topics.meaning: Basic substances are substances that are bitter, feel soapy, and turn red litmus blue.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Basic substances (also called bases) are another group of substances. They usually have a bitter taste and feel soapy or slippery to touch. Examples include baking soda solution, soap, detergent, lime water and milk of magnesia. Like acids, bases can be harmful and should never be tasted; we use indicators to test them safely. Bases are used in many everyday products such as soaps and cleaning agents. A key property of basic substances is that they turn red litmus paper blue, which helps us identify them.

topics.meaning: Neutral substances are neither acidic nor basic and do not change the colour of litmus.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Some substances are neither acidic nor basic; these are called neutral substances. Common examples are pure water, common salt solution and sugar solution. Because they are neutral, they do not change the colour of either red or blue litmus paper. Neutral substances are an important group because they help us understand the 'middle' between acids and bases. When an acid and a base react together in the right amounts, they can also form a neutral substance. Recognising neutral substances completes our picture of how substances can be classified.

topics.meaning: An indicator is a substance that changes colour to show whether a substance is acidic or basic.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Since tasting substances is unsafe, we use indicators to test whether something is acidic or basic. An indicator is a special substance that changes its colour depending on whether it is added to an acid or a base. By watching the colour change, we can tell the nature of a substance safely. There are many indicators, some made in laboratories and many found in nature. Indicators are very useful in science, in industries, and even at home, for example to test soil or water. The most common indicator used in schools is litmus.

topics.meaning: Litmus is a natural indicator that turns red in acids and blue in bases.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Litmus is the most commonly used indicator. It is obtained from a plant-like organism called lichen. Litmus is available as a solution and as paper in two colours, red and blue. The rule is simple: an acid turns blue litmus red, while a base turns red litmus blue. A neutral substance does not change the colour of either. So, to test a substance, we dip litmus paper into it and watch the colour change. Litmus is popular because it is easy to use, safe and gives a clear result, making it a reliable tool to identify acids and bases.

topics.meaning: Many indicators come from nature, such as turmeric and the China rose, which change colour in acids and bases.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Besides litmus, several indicators come from natural sources and can be made at home. Turmeric is a yellow natural indicator: it stays yellow in acids but turns red (or reddish-brown) in bases, which is why a turmeric stain on cloth may turn red when soap is applied. The China rose (red hibiscus) extract is another natural indicator: it turns acids pink or magenta and turns bases green. Red cabbage juice and the petals of some flowers can also act as indicators. These natural indicators show that science is all around us and can be explored using everyday materials.

topics.meaning: Neutralisation is the reaction between an acid and a base that cancels their effects and forms a neutral substance.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When an acid and a base are mixed together in the right amounts, they react and cancel out each other's properties. This reaction is called neutralisation. In neutralisation, the acid and base react to form a salt and water, which together are neutral, and the reaction usually releases heat. So a strong acid is no longer acidic and a strong base is no longer basic after they have neutralised each other. We can show neutralisation using an indicator, which changes back to a neutral colour. Neutralisation is one of the most important reactions in chemistry and daily life.

topics.meaning: Neutralisation is useful in many daily situations, such as treating acidity, soil and stings.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Neutralisation is not just a laboratory reaction; it is used in many everyday situations. When we have acidity (too much acid in the stomach), we take a mild base called an antacid to neutralise the extra acid and get relief. Farmers add lime (a base) to soil that is too acidic, or gypsum to soil that is too basic, to make it suitable for crops. The sting of an ant or a bee is acidic, so it can be soothed by rubbing on a mild base like baking soda; a wasp sting is basic and is soothed with a mild acid like vinegar. Factories also neutralise their acidic or basic waste before releasing it. These examples show how useful neutralisation is.

topics.meaning: An electric cell is a device that provides electricity by changing chemical energy into electrical energy.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An electric cell is a small device that stores chemical energy and changes it into electrical energy to make things work, such as a torch, a clock or a toy. A cell has two ends, called terminals: a positive terminal (+) and a negative terminal (-). The positive terminal usually has a small metal cap. When the cell is connected properly in a circuit, electricity flows out and back, making devices work. After some time the chemicals in the cell are used up, and the cell can no longer provide electricity. Cells are a common source of electricity for small devices.
topics.easyExplanation: An electric cell turns chemical energy into electricity. It has a positive (+) and a negative (-) terminal and powers small devices like torches and toys.

topics.meaning: A battery is made by joining two or more electric cells together to provide more electricity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A single cell may not give enough electricity for some devices, so two or more cells are joined together to form a battery. To make a battery, the cells are connected so that the positive terminal of one cell touches the negative terminal of the next. A battery provides more power than a single cell, which is why many devices like torches, transistors and toys use batteries. The cells must be placed the right way for the battery to work. Batteries are a common and convenient source of electricity for portable devices.

topics.meaning: An electric bulb gives light when electricity passes through its thin wire, called the filament.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An electric bulb is a device that gives light using electricity. Inside the bulb there is a thin coiled wire called the filament, which is connected to two terminals at the base of the bulb. When electricity passes through the filament, the filament becomes very hot and glows, giving out light. The filament is enclosed in a glass case to protect it. If the filament breaks, the bulb is said to be 'fused' and does not glow even when connected, because the path for electricity is broken. The bulb is a common device that turns electrical energy into light.

topics.meaning: An electric circuit is a complete path through which electricity flows from a cell to a device and back.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An electric circuit is the path along which electricity flows. A simple circuit is made of a cell (the source of electricity), connecting wires, and a device such as a bulb. The electricity, called electric current, flows out from the positive terminal of the cell, through the wires and the device, and back into the negative terminal. For the current to flow, the path must be complete and unbroken. When the circuit is complete and the bulb glows, it shows that current is flowing through the circuit. Understanding circuits is the key to understanding how electrical devices work.

topics.meaning: A closed circuit is a complete circuit in which current flows, while an open circuit is broken so no current flows.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An electric circuit can be either closed or open. A closed circuit is one in which the path is complete and unbroken, so electric current flows and the bulb glows. An open circuit is one in which the path is broken somewhere, for example a wire is disconnected or a switch is off, so no current flows and the bulb does not glow. By opening and closing a circuit, we can turn a device off and on. Knowing the difference between open and closed circuits helps us understand why a device sometimes works and sometimes does not.

topics.meaning: A circuit diagram uses standard symbols to show an electric circuit in a simple, clear way.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Drawing the actual components of a circuit takes time and can be unclear, so scientists use a circuit diagram, which shows the circuit using simple standard symbols. Each component has its own symbol: there are symbols for a cell, a battery, a bulb, a wire, a switch (open or closed) and other parts. The wires are drawn as straight lines connecting the symbols. Using these symbols, anyone can quickly draw and understand a circuit. Learning the standard symbols is important so that circuit diagrams can be read and drawn correctly by everyone.

topics.meaning: A switch is a device used to open or close a circuit, turning a device off or on.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A switch is a simple but very useful device that is used to turn electrical devices off and on. A switch works by opening or closing the circuit. When the switch is in the 'on' position, it closes the circuit, completing the path so that current flows and the device works. When the switch is 'off', it opens the circuit, breaking the path so that no current flows and the device stops. We can make a simple switch using two drawing pins and a safety pin. Switches are found everywhere, controlling lights, fans and almost every electrical device in our homes.
topics.easyExplanation: A switch turns a device off or on by opening or closing the circuit. 'On' closes the circuit (current flows); 'off' opens it (current stops).

topics.meaning: Conductors allow electricity to pass through them, while insulators do not.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Materials can be grouped by whether they let electricity pass. Materials that allow electric current to flow through them are called conductors; most metals, like copper, aluminium and iron, are good conductors, which is why wires are made of metal. Materials that do not allow current to flow are called insulators; examples are plastic, rubber, wood, glass and air. We can test a material by placing it in a circuit and seeing whether the bulb glows. Conductors are used to carry electricity, while insulators are used to cover wires and make switch handles, keeping us safe from electric shocks.

chapters.chapter: The World of Metals and Non-metals

topics.meaning: Materials can be classified into two main groups, metals and non-metals, based on their properties.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The world around us is made of many different materials, and to study them easily we group them. One important way is to classify materials as metals and non-metals based on their properties. Metals are materials like iron, copper, aluminium, gold and silver, while non-metals are materials like carbon, sulphur, oxygen and phosphorus. The two groups behave very differently: metals share a set of common properties, and non-metals share another. By testing properties such as shine, hardness and how they behave when hammered or heated, we can decide whether a material is a metal or a non-metal.
topics.easyExplanation: Materials are grouped into metals (like iron, copper, gold) and non-metals (like carbon, sulphur, oxygen). The two groups have different properties.

topics.meaning: Metals are usually shiny (have lustre) and hard, which are two of their key physical properties.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Metals have a set of physical properties that help us recognise them. One is lustre: metals have a shiny surface, especially when freshly cut or polished, which is why gold and silver are used in jewellery. Most metals are also hard, meaning they are not easily scratched or pressed and can bear weight, which is why they are used to make tools and machines. (A few metals like sodium are soft, but most are hard.) Non-metals, by contrast, are usually dull (not shiny) and soft or brittle. Lustre and hardness are among the first properties we notice in metals.

topics.meaning: Metals are malleable (can be beaten into sheets) and ductile (can be drawn into wires).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two special properties of metals are malleability and ductility. Malleability means that metals can be beaten into thin sheets without breaking; this is why thin sheets of aluminium and silver foil can be made and used to wrap food. Ductility means that metals can be drawn out into thin wires; this is why copper and aluminium are made into electrical wires. These properties make metals very useful, as they can be shaped into many forms. Non-metals do not have these properties: they are usually brittle, meaning they break easily into pieces when hammered, like a piece of coal or chalk.

topics.meaning: Metals are sonorous (make a ringing sound) and are good conductors of heat and electricity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two more properties of metals are sonority and conductivity. Sonority means that metals produce a ringing sound when struck, which is why bells and many musical instruments are made of metal. Conductivity means that metals are good conductors of heat and electricity, allowing heat and electric current to pass easily through them; this is why cooking utensils and electrical wires are made of metals. Non-metals are usually not sonorous, do not make a ringing sound, and are poor conductors of heat and electricity (they are insulators), like wood and plastic. These properties decide many of the uses of metals.

topics.meaning: Non-metals are usually dull, soft or brittle, non-sonorous and poor conductors, and are very different from metals.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Non-metals are the materials that do not have metallic properties. They are usually dull (not shiny), though there are a few exceptions. They are generally soft, or if solid, brittle, breaking into pieces when hammered rather than flattening. Non-metals are non-sonorous, so they do not make a ringing sound, and they are poor conductors of heat and electricity, behaving as insulators. Non-metals exist as solids (like carbon and sulphur), liquids (like bromine) or gases (like oxygen and nitrogen). Even though they differ greatly from metals, non-metals are extremely important and essential to life.

topics.meaning: Many metals react with air and moisture; iron, for example, rusts to form a reddish-brown layer.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Metals can react chemically with air, especially with the oxygen and moisture in it. The most common example is the rusting of iron. When iron is left exposed to both air (oxygen) and moisture (water) for some time, a reddish-brown layer called rust forms on its surface. Rust is a new substance that slowly eats away and weakens the iron object. Rusting needs both air and water; if either is missing, iron does not rust easily. Rusting can be prevented by painting, oiling, greasing or coating iron with another metal (galvanising), which keep air and moisture away.

topics.topic: Reaction of Metals with Water and Acids
topics.meaning: Metals react differently with water and acids, and these reactions show how reactive a metal is.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Besides air, metals also react with water and with acids, but not all metals react the same way. Some very reactive metals, like sodium, react vigorously even with cold water. Others react slowly or only with hot water or steam, while some metals like gold do not react with water at all. Many metals also react with acids, often producing hydrogen gas, which burns with a 'pop' sound. The way a metal reacts shows how reactive it is: some metals are very reactive and others are not. We must handle these reactions carefully, as some can be dangerous. These reactions help us understand and use metals safely.
topics.easyExplanation: Metals react differently with water and acids. Very reactive metals like sodium react fast; others react slowly or not at all. Reaction with acids often gives hydrogen gas.

topics.meaning: Metals and non-metals are used in countless ways in daily life and industry, based on their properties.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Both metals and non-metals are extremely useful because of their special properties. Metals are used to make tools, machines, vehicles, utensils, electrical wires, bridges, coins and jewellery, because they are strong, can be shaped, conduct electricity and shine. Non-metals are equally important: oxygen is essential for breathing and burning, carbon is found in all living things and in fuels, nitrogen and phosphorus are used in fertilisers, and non-metals are also used in medicines, crackers and water purification (chlorine). So both groups play vital roles, and whether we choose a metal or a non-metal depends on the properties needed for the job.

chapters.chapter: Changes Around Us: Physical and Chemical

topics.meaning: Changes happen all around us all the time, and we can study and classify them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many changes take place around us every day. Ice melts into water, milk turns into curd, paper burns, plants grow, and the seasons change. A change means that something becomes different from what it was before, in its shape, size, state, colour or nature. By studying changes, we can understand what happens and why. Scientists classify changes in different ways, for example as physical or chemical, reversible or irreversible, and slow or fast. Learning about changes helps us make use of helpful changes and prevent harmful ones in our daily lives.
topics.easyExplanation: Changes happen all around us, like ice melting or paper burning. A change means something becomes different. We classify changes to understand them.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many changes happen around us every day.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A change means something becomes different in shape, size, state, colour or nature.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Studying changes helps us understand what happens and why.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Classifying changes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Changes are grouped as physical/chemical, reversible/irreversible, slow/fast.

topics.meaning: A physical change is one in which no new substance is formed and which is often reversible.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a physical change, a substance may change its shape, size or state, but no new substance is formed, and the substance remains the same material. For example, when ice melts into water, both are still water; when we tear paper, the pieces are still paper; and when sugar dissolves in water, it is still sugar. Other examples are stretching a rubber band, folding paper, and the freezing of water. Physical changes are usually easy to reverse, for example by freezing water back into ice. Because no new substance is formed, the original substance can usually be got back.

topics.meaning: A chemical change is one in which one or more new substances with new properties are formed.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a chemical change, one or more new substances are formed that have different properties from the original substances, and the change is usually difficult to reverse. For example, when paper or wood burns, it turns into ash, gases and smoke, which are new substances; when iron rusts, a new substance (rust) is formed; and when milk turns into curd, a new substance is formed. Cooking of food and the souring of milk are also chemical changes. In every chemical change, the original substance is changed into something new, which is why chemical changes are very important in nature, in our bodies and in industries.
topics.easyExplanation: In a chemical change, a new substance forms and it is usually hard to reverse, like burning paper, rusting iron, cooking food or milk turning to curd.

topics.meaning: A chemical change can often be recognised by signs such as change in colour, gas, smell, heat or light.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can tell that a chemical change has happened by looking for certain signs. These signs include: the formation of a new substance; a change in colour (like iron turning into reddish-brown rust); the giving off of a gas (like bubbles when an antacid is dropped in water); a change in smell (like milk going sour); a change in temperature, with heat being given out or taken in; and sometimes light being produced (like in burning). One or more of these signs usually appear in a chemical change. By noticing these signs, we can decide whether a change is chemical rather than physical.
topics.easyExplanation: Signs of a chemical change include a new substance, a colour change, a gas being given off, a smell, and heat or light. These help us spot a chemical change.

topics.meaning: Reversible changes can be undone, while irreversible changes cannot easily be undone.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Changes can also be grouped by whether they can be reversed. A reversible change is one that can be changed back to the original state, like the melting and freezing of water, folding and unfolding paper, or stretching and releasing a rubber band. An irreversible change is one that cannot easily be changed back, like burning a candle, cooking an egg, or the ripening of fruit. In general, many physical changes are reversible, while most chemical changes are irreversible, because a new substance is formed. Knowing whether a change is reversible helps us decide whether we can get the original substance back.
topics.easyExplanation: Reversible changes can be undone (like melting and freezing water); irreversible changes cannot (like burning or cooking). Most chemical changes are irreversible.

topics.meaning: Some changes are permanent (lasting), while others are temporary (can change back).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Another way to look at changes is whether they are permanent or temporary. A permanent change is one that lasts and cannot be undone, so the substance does not return to its original form; for example, when wood is burnt into ash, it cannot become wood again. A temporary change is one that can be undone, so the substance can return to its original form; for example, when ice melts into water, it can be frozen back into ice. Permanent changes are usually chemical changes, while many temporary changes are physical. Understanding whether a change is permanent helps us know whether we can recover the original substance.
topics.easyExplanation: Permanent changes last and cannot be undone (wood burning to ash). Temporary changes can be undone (ice melting and refreezing).

topics.meaning: Some changes are desirable (useful) and others are undesirable (harmful) to us.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Changes can also be grouped by whether they are good or bad for us. A desirable change is one that is useful or wanted, like the cooking of food, the ripening of fruit, the making of curd from milk, or the growth of crops. An undesirable change is one that is harmful or unwanted, like the souring of milk, the rotting of food, or the rusting of iron, which we try to prevent. The same change may be desirable in one situation and undesirable in another. Recognising desirable and undesirable changes helps us make use of the helpful ones and prevent the harmful ones.

topics.topic: Slow and Fast Changes in Nature
topics.meaning: Changes in nature can be fast or very slow, and slow natural changes shape the Earth over long times.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Changes in nature happen at different speeds. Some changes are fast and happen quickly, like the bursting of a cracker, the burning of paper, or a flash of lightning. Other changes are very slow and take a long time, sometimes years or even thousands of years, like the rusting of iron, the ripening of fruit, the weathering of rocks into soil, and the formation of fossil fuels. The slow changes in nature, such as the breaking down of rocks and the formation of soil, are very important because they shape the Earth's surface over long periods. Recognising slow and fast changes helps us understand the natural processes around us.
topics.easyExplanation: Changes can be fast (a cracker bursting) or very slow (rusting, rock weathering, soil formation). Slow natural changes shape the Earth over long times.

chapters.chapter: Adolescence: A Stage of Growth and Change

topics.meaning: Adolescence is the stage of life, usually from about 10 to 19 years, when a child gradually grows into an adult.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Adolescence is an important stage of life, usually between about 10 and 19 years of age, during which a child slowly grows and develops into an adult. It is a period of many changes, in the body, in feelings and in behaviour. These changes happen because of special chemical messengers in the body called hormones. The changes do not happen all at once; they take place gradually over several years, and they happen at slightly different ages and rates for different individuals. Adolescence is a completely normal and healthy part of growing up that everyone goes through.
topics.easyExplanation: Adolescence (about 10 to 19 years) is when a child grows into an adult, with many changes in body, feelings and behaviour, caused by hormones.

topics.meaning: During adolescence, the body grows rapidly in height and weight.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: One of the most noticeable changes during adolescence is rapid physical growth. There is a sudden increase in height as the long bones of the arms and legs grow longer, and there is also an increase in weight and changes in body shape. This rapid growth is sometimes called a 'growth spurt'. The rate of growth is different for different people, and girls often begin their growth spurt a little earlier than boys, though boys may grow taller later. After adolescence, this rapid growth slows down and the body reaches its adult height. Good nutrition is very important during this period of fast growth.
topics.easyExplanation: During adolescence the body grows fast in height and weight, called a growth spurt. Girls often start earlier than boys. Good food is important for this growth.

topics.meaning: During adolescence, the body develops in ways that show it is maturing towards adulthood.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: During adolescence, the body develops in several ways that show it is maturing into an adult. These changes are controlled by hormones. They include changes in body shape, the growth of hair in new areas of the body, changes in the skin and voice (the voice becomes deeper, especially in boys), and the development of the reproductive organs. Boys and girls go through some changes that are common and some that are different. All these changes are a normal and natural part of growing up, and they happen gradually. Understanding them helps adolescents feel comfortable about the changes in their bodies.
topics.easyExplanation: During adolescence the body matures: changes in body shape, hair growth, skin, voice (deeper in boys) and reproductive organs. These changes are normal and caused by hormones.

topics.meaning: Adolescents experience emotional and behavioural changes, including new feelings and mood swings.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Along with physical changes, adolescents also go through emotional and behavioural changes. They may feel more independent and want to take their own decisions, have new and stronger emotions, experience mood swings (quick changes in mood), and become more self-conscious about their appearance. They may also want more privacy and form stronger friendships. These changes are caused partly by hormones and partly by growing up and facing new situations. It is completely normal to feel confused or moody at times. Talking to parents, teachers or trusted elders, and treating others with respect and kindness, helps adolescents handle these changes in a healthy way.

topics.meaning: Because of rapid growth, adolescents need a balanced and nutritious diet.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Because the body grows so rapidly during adolescence, good nutrition becomes very important. Adolescents need a balanced diet that contains all the nutrients in the right amounts: proteins for growth and body-building (from milk, eggs, pulses, meat), carbohydrates and fats for energy, and plenty of vitamins and minerals (from fruits and vegetables) to stay healthy. Iron is especially important during these years. Adolescents should avoid eating too much junk food, which is low in nutrients and can lead to poor health. Eating a variety of healthy foods and drinking enough water supports the fast growth and keeps the body strong.

topics.meaning: Good personal hygiene is very important during adolescence because the body changes and sweats more.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: During adolescence, taking care of personal hygiene becomes especially important. As the body matures, adolescents sweat more, and the skin may become oily, so keeping the body clean helps prevent body odour, skin problems and infections. Good personal hygiene includes bathing regularly, washing the body and hair, keeping the hands and nails clean, brushing the teeth twice a day, and wearing clean clothes. Girls need to take special care of cleanliness during menstruation. Keeping clean not only keeps adolescents healthy but also makes them feel fresh and confident. Good hygiene habits formed now last a lifetime.

topics.meaning: Regular physical activity and enough rest keep an adolescent's body and mind healthy.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Regular physical activity is very important during adolescence. Activities like playing games, sports, running, cycling, yoga and exercise keep the body fit, strong and flexible, help in proper growth, and keep the heart and lungs healthy. Physical activity also keeps the mind fresh, reduces stress, and helps adolescents feel happy. Along with activity, the body needs enough rest and sleep to recover and grow; adolescents need adequate sleep each night. A balance of activity and rest, avoiding too much screen time, keeps the growing body and mind in good health during this important stage.

topics.meaning: Adolescents should follow healthy habits, think critically and avoid false beliefs and harmful habits.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: During adolescence it is important to follow good habits and to think clearly about health. Adolescents should keep a balanced social life, spend time with good friends and family, and treat everyone, including those who are different, with respect. They should avoid harmful habits and substances that damage health. It is also important not to believe false ideas, or myths, about the body and about food; for example, some people wrongly believe that certain normal changes are 'problems'. Adolescents should get correct information from reliable sources like teachers, doctors and good books, and feel free to ask trusted elders their questions. Healthy habits and correct knowledge keep adolescents well in body and mind.

topics.meaning: Heat is a form of energy that flows because of a difference in temperature, and temperature tells how hot or cold something is.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Heat is a form of energy. Temperature is a measure of how hot or cold an object is, measured with a thermometer in degrees Celsius. Heat and temperature are related but different: heat is the energy that flows, while temperature tells us the degree of hotness. The important rule is that heat always flows from a hotter object to a colder object, until both reach the same temperature. For example, a hot cup of tea loses heat to the cooler air and cools down. Understanding heat and temperature is the basis for understanding how heat moves, or is transferred, in nature.

topics.meaning: Conduction is the transfer of heat through a solid from its hotter part to its colder part, without the material moving.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Conduction is the way heat travels through solids. When one end of a solid object is heated, the heat passes from the hot end to the cold end through the material, while the material itself does not move from its place. For example, if one end of a metal spoon is placed in hot tea, the other end also becomes warm after some time. The particles of the solid pass on the heat from one to the next. Conduction is the main way heat moves through solids, and it is important in cooking, where heat passes through metal utensils to the food.

topics.meaning: Conductors of heat allow heat to pass easily, while insulators do not.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Materials differ in how well they conduct heat. Materials that allow heat to pass through them easily are called good conductors of heat; most metals, like copper, aluminium and iron, are good conductors, which is why cooking utensils are made of metal. Materials that do not allow heat to pass easily are called poor conductors or insulators; examples are wood, plastic, rubber and air, which is why the handles of utensils are made of wood or plastic to protect our hands. Insulators are also used in clothing (wool traps air to keep us warm) and in keeping things hot or cold. Knowing which materials conduct heat helps us use them wisely.

topics.meaning: Convection is the transfer of heat in liquids and gases by the actual movement of the heated material.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Convection is the way heat travels through liquids and gases (fluids). When a fluid is heated, the hot part becomes lighter and rises up, while the cooler, heavier part sinks down to take its place. This sets up a continuous circular movement called a convection current, which carries heat throughout the fluid. For example, when water in a pan is heated, convection currents make all the water hot, and warm air in a room rises while cool air sinks. Convection is the main way heat moves through liquids and gases and is important in heating water, in the air around us, and in many natural events.

topics.meaning: Land and sea breezes are winds caused by convection, because land and sea heat and cool at different rates.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Land and sea breezes are natural winds caused by convection near coasts. Land heats up and cools down faster than the sea. During the day, the land becomes hotter than the sea, so the warm air over the land rises and cooler air from the sea moves in to take its place; this cool wind blowing from the sea to the land is called the sea breeze. At night, the land cools down faster than the sea, so the air over the sea is warmer and rises, and cooler air from the land moves towards the sea; this wind blowing from the land to the sea is called the land breeze. These breezes are everyday examples of convection in the air.

topics.meaning: Radiation is the transfer of heat without any material medium, and it is how heat reaches us from the Sun.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Radiation is the third way heat travels, and unlike conduction and convection, it does not need any material to pass through. In radiation, heat travels as invisible rays and can pass even through empty space (a vacuum). This is how the heat of the Sun reaches the Earth across millions of kilometres of empty space, and how we feel the warmth of a fire or a heater even from a distance without touching it. Dark and dull surfaces absorb more heat radiation and also give it out faster, while light and shiny surfaces reflect heat radiation. Radiation is an important way heat moves in nature and in our daily lives.

topics.topic: Heat Transfer in the Water Cycle
topics.meaning: The Sun's heat drives the water cycle by evaporating water, which later condenses and falls as rain.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Heat transfer plays a key role in the water cycle, the continuous movement of water between the Earth and the sky. The Sun's heat, reaching the Earth by radiation, warms the water in oceans, rivers and lakes, causing it to evaporate into water vapour that rises into the air. As the vapour rises higher, it reaches cooler air and condenses into tiny droplets, forming clouds. When the droplets grow heavy, they fall as rain, snow or hail (precipitation), and the water returns to the Earth. Without the Sun's heat driving evaporation, the water cycle, which gives us rain and fresh water, could not happen.
topics.easyExplanation: The Sun's heat evaporates water into vapour, which rises, cools, condenses into clouds, and falls as rain. Heat transfer drives the whole water cycle.

topics.topic: Heat and the Shaping of the Earth
topics.meaning: Heat transfer in nature helps shape the Earth's surface through processes like weathering and erosion.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Heat plays a part in slowly shaping the surface of the Earth. During the day, the Sun's heat makes rocks expand, and at night, as they cool, they contract. This repeated heating and cooling, over a long time, causes rocks to crack and break into smaller pieces; this slow breaking down of rocks is called weathering, and it helps form soil. The broken pieces of rock and soil are then carried away by wind and flowing water; this carrying away is called erosion. Heat also drives winds and the water cycle, which cause weathering and erosion. In these ways, heat transfer slowly changes mountains, rivers and the land over very long periods.

topics.meaning: Long ago, people measured time using natural repeating events and simple devices.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Since ancient times, people have needed to measure time. They did this by observing events in nature that repeat at regular intervals, such as the rising and setting of the Sun (giving the day), the changing phases of the Moon (giving the month), and the changing seasons (giving the year). People also invented simple devices to measure smaller intervals of time, such as the sundial, which uses the shadow of the Sun, the water clock, which uses the steady dripping of water, and the sand clock (hourglass). These early methods were useful but not always very accurate. The need for more accurate timekeeping led to the discovery of better methods.

topics.meaning: A simple pendulum swings back and forth in a regular way and was an important discovery for measuring time.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A simple pendulum is a small heavy object (called the bob) hung from a fixed point by a thread, so that it can swing freely back and forth. When the bob is pulled to one side and released, it swings to and fro in a regular, repeating way. This regular swinging made the pendulum very useful for measuring time, and it led to the making of accurate pendulum clocks. The great scientist Galileo studied the swinging of the pendulum. The pendulum's regular motion is an example of oscillatory (to-and-fro) motion, and it forms the basis of measuring time accurately.

topics.meaning: The time period of a pendulum is the time it takes to complete one full swing, and it stays the same for a given pendulum.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The time period of a simple pendulum is the time it takes to complete one full to-and-fro swing (one oscillation). A very important property is that for a given pendulum, the time period stays the same for each swing, no matter how big or small the swing is. This is what makes the pendulum so useful for keeping time. The time period depends on the length of the pendulum: a longer pendulum has a longer time period (swings more slowly), and a shorter pendulum has a shorter time period (swings faster). By measuring the time for several swings and dividing, we can find the time period accurately.

topics.meaning: The SI unit of time is the second, and larger units include the minute, hour, day and year.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To measure time exactly, we use standard units. The SI (standard) unit of time is the second, written 's'. Larger units of time are related to the second: 1 minute = 60 seconds, 1 hour = 60 minutes, 1 day = 24 hours, and a year has about 365 days. Modern clocks and watches measure time very accurately in these units. We choose the right unit for each situation: seconds for a short race, minutes for a class, hours for a journey, and years for someone's age. Using standard units of time means a measurement of time means exactly the same to everyone everywhere.

topics.meaning: An object is in motion if its position changes with time, and at rest if its position does not change.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Some things around us stay in one place while others move. If the position of an object changes with respect to its surroundings as time passes, the object is said to be in motion. If its position does not change with time, the object is said to be at rest. Whether an object is at rest or in motion depends on the reference point we choose. For example, a passenger sitting in a moving bus is at rest with respect to the bus, but in motion with respect to the road and the trees outside. So motion and rest are described relative to a chosen reference point.
topics.easyExplanation: An object is in motion if its position changes with time, and at rest if it does not. Whether it moves depends on what we compare it with (the reference point).

topics.meaning: Speed is how fast an object moves, found by dividing the distance travelled by the time taken.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Speed tells us how fast or slow an object is moving. It is defined as the distance travelled by an object in a given time, and is calculated using the formula: speed = distance divided by time. The unit of speed depends on the units of distance and time, for example metres per second (m/s) or kilometres per hour (km/h). An object that covers more distance in the same time has a greater speed. Speed lets us compare how fast different objects move, such as a person walking, a cyclist and a car. Knowing speed is useful in everyday life for planning journeys and understanding motion.
topics.easyExplanation: Speed is how fast something moves: speed = distance ÷ time, measured in m/s or km/h. An object covering more distance in the same time has a greater speed.

topics.topic: Relationship between Speed, Distance and Time
topics.meaning: If we know any two of speed, distance and time, we can find the third using their relationship.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Speed, distance and time are connected by one simple relationship: speed = distance / time. From this, we can rearrange to find any one of the three quantities if we know the other two. So, distance = speed multiplied by time, and time = distance divided by speed. For example, if a car travels at a speed of 40 km/h for 2 hours, the distance covered is 40 multiplied by 2 = 80 km. This relationship is very useful for solving everyday problems about journeys, such as finding how long a trip will take or how far we will travel. Knowing this relationship helps us plan and understand motion.
topics.easyExplanation: Speed = distance ÷ time. From this: distance = speed x time, and time = distance ÷ speed. Knowing two of them lets us find the third.

topics.meaning: Motion is uniform when an object covers equal distances in equal times, and non-uniform when it does not.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When an object moves, its motion can be uniform or non-uniform. In uniform motion, the object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time, so its speed stays the same; for example, a car moving steadily at 40 km/h. In non-uniform motion, the object covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time, so its speed keeps changing; for example, a bus that speeds up and slows down in traffic. We can show motion using a distance-time graph, where a straight line shows uniform motion and a curved line shows non-uniform motion. Understanding these types helps us describe how objects really move.

topics.meaning: Nutrition is the process by which animals take in food and use it for energy, growth and repair.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: All animals need food to live, grow and stay healthy. Nutrition is the process of taking in food and using it. Since animals cannot make their own food like plants, they must take it from outside, which is why they are called consumers. The food contains nutrients, which give energy, help the body grow and repair damaged parts. The food taken in must be broken down into simpler substances that the body can absorb and use; this breaking down is called digestion. Different animals eat different kinds of food and have different ways of taking in and digesting it, suited to what they eat.

topics.meaning: In humans, digestion takes place in the digestive system, where food is broken down and absorbed.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In human beings, digestion takes place in a long tube called the digestive system, which includes the mouth, food pipe, stomach and intestines. In the mouth, food is chewed by the teeth and mixed with saliva, which begins digestion. The food then passes down the food pipe into the stomach, where it is churned and broken down further by digestive juices. From the stomach the food goes into the small intestine, where digestion is completed and the useful nutrients are absorbed into the blood. The undigested waste passes into the large intestine and is finally removed from the body. This whole process lets the body get nutrients from food.

topics.meaning: Different animals have different ways of taking in and digesting food, suited to what they eat.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Not all animals eat or digest food in the same way. Different animals have different feeding habits and special body parts to suit their food. Grass-eating animals (herbivores) like cows have flat teeth to grind grass and a special stomach to digest it; some, like cows, chew the cud, bringing food back up to chew again. Meat-eating animals (carnivores) like lions have sharp teeth to tear flesh. Some animals, like the amoeba, are tiny and take in food by surrounding it, while a snake swallows its prey whole. Birds have beaks suited to their food. These different methods show how animals are adapted to get nutrition from their particular food.
topics.easyExplanation: Different animals eat and digest in different ways: cows grind grass and chew cud, lions tear meat with sharp teeth, and the amoeba surrounds its food. Each is adapted to its food.

topics.meaning: Respiration is the process by which animals release energy from food using oxygen.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Respiration is the life process by which living things release the energy stored in their food. In respiration, oxygen taken in from the air is used to break down food inside the cells of the body, which releases energy and gives out carbon dioxide and water vapour. This energy is needed for all the activities of life, such as moving, growing and keeping the body warm. The taking in of oxygen and giving out of carbon dioxide is called breathing, which is part of respiration. All animals respire, because all of them need energy to live. Respiration goes on all the time, day and night.
topics.easyExplanation: Respiration is how animals get energy from food using oxygen, giving out carbon dioxide. The taking in of oxygen and giving out of carbon dioxide is breathing.

topics.meaning: Different animals breathe through different organs, such as lungs, gills, the skin or tiny tubes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Different animals have different organs for breathing, suited to where they live. Humans and many land animals, like dogs, cats and cows, breathe using lungs, which take in oxygen from the air. Fish and many water animals breathe using gills, which take in oxygen dissolved in water. Insects, like grasshoppers and cockroaches, breathe through a network of tiny tubes in their body. Earthworms and frogs (when in water) can breathe through their moist skin. So even though all animals respire, the organs they use to breathe are different and are adapted to their surroundings, on land or in water.

topics.meaning: In breathing, animals take in air rich in oxygen and give out air rich in carbon dioxide.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Breathing is the part of respiration in which air is taken in and given out. When we breathe in (inhale), we take in air that is rich in oxygen, which the body uses for respiration. When we breathe out (exhale), we give out air that is rich in carbon dioxide, the waste gas made during respiration. This swapping of oxygen for carbon dioxide is called the exchange of gases. We can show that exhaled air contains carbon dioxide, because it turns lime water milky. The number of times we breathe in and out in a minute is called the breathing rate, which increases when we exercise, because the body needs more energy and oxygen.

topics.meaning: In animals, the blood transports oxygen, food and other materials to all parts of the body.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In an animal's body, the materials taken in must be carried to every part. This carrying is done by the blood, which flows through the body in tubes called blood vessels, pushed by the heart. The blood carries oxygen from the breathing organs and digested food (nutrients) from the intestines to all the cells of the body, where they are used for energy and growth. The blood also carries away waste materials, like carbon dioxide, to be removed. In this way, the heart and blood form a transport system that keeps every part of the body supplied with what it needs and free from waste.

topics.meaning: Life processes like nutrition, respiration and transport work together to keep an animal alive and active.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The life processes of an animal, nutrition, digestion, respiration, breathing and transport, all work together to keep it alive. Nutrition and digestion provide the food and nutrients the body needs. Respiration releases the energy stored in this food, using oxygen taken in by breathing. The transport system (blood and heart) carries food, oxygen and waste to and from every part of the body. Together, these processes give the animal the energy and materials it needs to move, grow, respond and stay healthy, and they remove waste. If any of these processes stops, the animal cannot stay alive. This shows how all the life processes are connected and essential.

topics.topic: How Plants Make Their Own Food
topics.meaning: Green plants make their own food, which is why they are called producers.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Unlike animals, green plants do not eat food; instead, they make their own food. Because they produce food, plants are called producers, and they form the base of all food chains, since animals depend on plants for food directly or indirectly. Plants make their food in their green leaves through a process called photosynthesis, using sunlight, water and carbon dioxide. The food they make is a kind of sugar, which gives the plant energy and is also used to build the plant's body. Some of this food is stored in different parts of the plant, like roots, fruits and seeds, which we often eat.

topics.meaning: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make food using sunlight, water and carbon dioxide.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make their own food. It takes place mainly in the green leaves. For photosynthesis, plants need four things: sunlight (which provides energy), water (absorbed from the soil by the roots), carbon dioxide (taken from the air), and chlorophyll (the green substance in leaves). Using the energy of sunlight captured by chlorophyll, the leaves combine carbon dioxide and water to make food (a sugar) and release oxygen into the air. Photosynthesis is one of the most important processes on Earth, because it makes food for almost all living things and keeps the air balanced.

topics.topic: Chlorophyll and the Role of Leaves
topics.meaning: The green leaves, with their chlorophyll, are the food factories of the plant.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The leaves are often called the 'food factories' of a plant, because most photosynthesis happens in them. Leaves are usually broad and flat, which gives them a large surface to catch sunlight. They contain a green substance called chlorophyll, which gives leaves their green colour and captures the energy of sunlight needed for photosynthesis. Tiny veins in the leaf carry water to all parts of the leaf and carry away the food made. Because chlorophyll is essential, only the green parts of a plant can make food. The leaf is perfectly designed for its job of making food for the plant.

topics.meaning: Stomata are tiny pores on leaves through which plants exchange gases and release water vapour.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The leaves of a plant have very tiny pores on their surface called stomata. These pores are so small that they can only be seen under a microscope. Stomata play an important role: they allow the leaf to take in carbon dioxide from the air for photosynthesis, and to release the oxygen made during photosynthesis. Stomata also let out extra water from the plant as water vapour, a process called transpiration. The stomata can open and close to control these exchanges. Through stomata, the leaf is connected to the air, taking in and giving out gases, which is essential for the plant's life processes.

topics.topic: Transport of Water and Minerals in Plants
topics.meaning: Plants absorb water and minerals from the soil through their roots and transport them up to the leaves through the xylem.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A plant needs to carry water and minerals from the soil up to its leaves, where food is made. The roots of a plant absorb water and dissolved minerals from the soil. These then travel upwards through the stem to the leaves inside tube-like tissues called xylem, which run all the way from the roots to the leaves like pipes. The pull created when water evaporates from the leaves (transpiration) helps draw water up. In this way, every part of the plant, especially the leaves, gets the water and minerals it needs. The transport of water and minerals is essential for photosynthesis and the plant's growth.

topics.meaning: The food made in the leaves is transported to all parts of the plant through the phloem.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The food made in the leaves by photosynthesis must be carried to all the other parts of the plant, such as the stem, roots, flowers, fruits and seeds, which cannot make their own food. This food is transported through another set of tube-like tissues called phloem. Unlike xylem, which carries water and minerals only upwards, the phloem can carry food both up and down the plant, to wherever it is needed for energy, growth or storage. So the xylem and phloem together form the transport system of the plant. The transport of food ensures that every part of the plant is well supplied and can grow and stay alive.

topics.meaning: Plants also respire, taking in oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide to get energy from their food.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Like all living things, plants also respire to get energy from their food. During respiration, the cells of the plant take in oxygen and use it to break down food, releasing energy and giving out carbon dioxide. Respiration happens all the time, day and night, in all parts of the plant, including the leaves, stems and roots. Gases are exchanged through the stomata in the leaves and through small openings in the stems and roots. It is important to remember that photosynthesis (making food) happens only in the presence of light, while respiration (using food for energy) goes on all the time.

topics.topic: Other Modes of Nutrition in Plants
topics.meaning: Some plants get food in special ways, such as insect-eating plants and plants that depend on others.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although most green plants make their own food by photosynthesis, a few plants get their food in special ways. Some plants, like the pitcher plant and the Venus flytrap, grow in soil that lacks certain nutrients, so they trap and digest insects to get those nutrients; these are called insectivorous (insect-eating) plants. Some plants, like the dodder (amarbel), cannot make their own food and instead take food from another plant on which they grow; these are called parasitic plants. Some plants live together with tiny organisms and help each other. These special ways of getting food show how plants are adapted to survive in different conditions.

topics.meaning: Objects that give out their own light are luminous, while those seen by reflected light are non-luminous.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We are able to see things because of light. Some objects give out their own light; these are called luminous objects, for example the Sun, the stars, a lighted bulb, a candle flame and a glowing firefly. Other objects do not give out their own light but are seen because they reflect light falling on them from a source; these are called non-luminous objects, for example the Moon, a book, a chair and a wall. We see non-luminous objects only when light from a luminous source falls on them and bounces off into our eyes. Without any source of light, we cannot see anything.

topics.topic: Light Travels in a Straight Line
topics.meaning: Light travels in a straight line, which explains shadows and many things we see.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An important property of light is that it travels in a straight line. We can show this by looking at a candle flame through three cardboard sheets with holes: we can see the flame only when the holes are in a straight line, but not when one sheet is moved, because light cannot bend around the corner. The straight-line travel of light explains why we cannot see around bends, why shadows form, and how a pinhole camera works. The straight path along which light travels is sometimes shown as a 'ray' of light. This simple property is the basis for understanding shadows, reflection and images.
topics.easyExplanation: Light travels in a straight line. This is why we can't see around corners, why shadows form, and how a pinhole camera works.

topics.meaning: Objects are classified by how much light passes through them as transparent, translucent or opaque.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Different objects let different amounts of light pass through them. Transparent objects allow almost all the light to pass through, so we can see clearly through them; examples are clear glass, clean water and air. Translucent objects allow only some light to pass through, so we can see only blurred or unclear images through them; examples are frosted glass, oily paper and thin cloth. Opaque objects do not allow any light to pass through, so we cannot see through them at all; examples are wood, metal, stone and a book. Opaque objects block light completely and form clear, dark shadows, while transparent objects hardly form shadows.

topics.meaning: A shadow forms when an opaque object blocks light travelling in a straight line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A shadow is the dark area that forms when an opaque object comes in the path of light. Because light travels in a straight line, the opaque object blocks it, and a dark shape of the object is formed on the other side, on a screen or the ground. To form a shadow, we need three things: a source of light, an opaque object, and a screen to catch the shadow. A shadow is always the same shape as the outline of the object, and it is always black or dark, no matter what colour the object is. The size of the shadow changes with the position of the light; for example, the Sun makes long shadows in the morning and evening and short shadows at noon.

topics.meaning: Reflection is the bouncing back of light when it falls on a shiny, smooth surface like a mirror.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When light falls on a shiny, smooth surface such as a mirror, polished metal or still water, it bounces back; this bouncing back of light is called reflection. A mirror reflects almost all the light that falls on it, which is why we can see a clear image in it. The light that falls on the mirror is called the incident ray, and the light that bounces back is called the reflected ray. Reflection follows a rule: light reflects off a smooth surface in a regular way, which is why mirrors form clear images. Rough surfaces also reflect light, but in a scattered way, so they do not form clear images. Reflection helps us see objects and is used in mirrors.

topics.topic: Images Formed in a Plane Mirror
topics.meaning: A plane mirror forms an image that is the same size, upright, behind the mirror and laterally inverted.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A plane mirror is a flat, shiny surface that forms a clear image of objects placed in front of it. The image formed in a plane mirror has special features. It is the same size as the object. It is upright (the right way up). It appears to be as far behind the mirror as the object is in front of it. It cannot be caught on a screen, so it is called a virtual image. And it is laterally inverted, meaning the left and right sides are exchanged, so your right hand appears as the left hand of your image. This is why the word 'AMBULANCE' is written reversed on vehicles, so it reads correctly in a mirror. These features are the same for every plane mirror.

topics.meaning: A pinhole camera uses a tiny hole to form a small, inverted image, showing that light travels in straight lines.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A pinhole camera is a simple device that forms an image using a tiny hole instead of a lens. It can be made from a box with a small pinhole on one side and a screen (like tracing paper) on the opposite side. Light from a bright object passes in straight lines through the pinhole and forms an image on the screen. The image formed is upside down (inverted), smaller than the object, and shows the colours of the object. The pinhole camera works because light travels in straight lines, so light from the top of the object reaches the bottom of the screen and the other way round. It clearly demonstrates the straight-line travel of light.

topics.meaning: Mirrors and reflection are used in many useful ways in daily life and devices.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Reflection of light by mirrors is very useful in our daily lives. Plane mirrors are used at home for us to see ourselves, and by barbers and tailors. They are used in vehicles as rear-view mirrors so drivers can see what is behind them. Mirrors are used to make periscopes, which let us see over walls or around corners, used in submarines and by soldiers. Curved mirrors are used in torches, car headlights and vehicle side mirrors. Reflection also helps make kaleidoscopes, which create beautiful patterns using mirrors. Many of these uses depend on the way mirrors reflect light to form images, showing how useful reflection is.

topics.topic: The Night Sky and Celestial Bodies
topics.meaning: The objects we see in the sky, such as the Sun, Moon, stars and planets, are called celestial bodies.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we look at the sky, we see many objects, both during the day and at night. All the objects in the sky, such as the Sun, the Moon, the stars, the planets and comets, are called celestial bodies or heavenly bodies. The Sun is a star that gives us light and heat. The stars are huge balls of hot, glowing gas that look tiny because they are very far away. The planets, including our Earth, move around the Sun. The Moon is the Earth's natural satellite. By observing the night sky and learning about these celestial bodies, we can understand the Earth's place in space and the movements that give us day, night, months and seasons.

topics.topic: Rotation of the Earth: Day and Night
topics.meaning: The Earth spins on its axis, which causes day and night.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Earth spins, or rotates, on its own imaginary axis like a top, taking about 24 hours to complete one full rotation. Because of this rotation, at any moment one half of the Earth faces the Sun and has daytime, with light and warmth, while the other half faces away from the Sun and has night-time, which is dark. As the Earth keeps turning, places move from day into night and from night into day. This is why the Sun appears to rise in the east and set in the west, even though it is really the Earth that is moving. The rotation of the Earth gives us the regular cycle of day and night.

topics.topic: Revolution of the Earth and Seasons
topics.meaning: The Earth revolves around the Sun once a year, and its tilt causes the seasons.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Besides spinning on its axis, the Earth also moves around the Sun in a fixed path called its orbit; this movement is called revolution, and it takes about 365 days (one year) to complete. The Earth's axis is tilted, and because of this tilt, different parts of the Earth receive different amounts of sunlight at different times of the year. This is what causes the seasons, summer, winter and others. When a part of the Earth is tilted towards the Sun it receives more direct sunlight and has summer; when it is tilted away it receives less sunlight and has winter. So the revolution of the tilted Earth around the Sun gives us the changing seasons through the year.
topics.easyExplanation: The Earth revolves around the Sun once a year (about 365 days). Because the Earth's axis is tilted, different parts get different sunlight, causing the seasons.

topics.meaning: The Moon is the Earth's natural satellite, which moves around the Earth and shines by reflecting sunlight.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Moon is the Earth's only natural satellite, which means it is a body that moves around the Earth. The Moon takes about one month (about 27 to 29 days) to go once around the Earth. The Moon does not give out its own light; we see it because it reflects the light of the Sun that falls on it. The Moon's surface is rocky and covered with craters, and it has no air or water, so no life can live there. As the Moon moves around the Earth, we see different amounts of its sunlit part, which makes the Moon appear to change its shape. The Moon is the closest celestial body to the Earth.

topics.meaning: The Moon appears to change its shape over a month because we see different amounts of its sunlit part.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Moon appears to change its shape from night to night; these different shapes are called the phases of the Moon. The Moon does not actually change shape; it appears to, because of the way we see its sunlit half from the Earth as it moves around us. The Sun always lights up one half of the Moon, but from the Earth we see different amounts of this lit half on different days. The phases go from the new Moon (when we cannot see the Moon at all), to a thin crescent, to the half Moon, to the bright full Moon (when we see the whole lit half), and then back again. This cycle of phases repeats about every month.

topics.meaning: A solar eclipse happens when the Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth, blocking the Sun's light.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon comes exactly between the Sun and the Earth, so that the Moon blocks the Sun's light from reaching part of the Earth. During a solar eclipse, the Sun appears to be fully or partly hidden for the people who are in the Moon's shadow, and it can become dark in the middle of the day. A solar eclipse always happens on a new Moon day, because that is when the Moon is between the Sun and the Earth. We must never look directly at the Sun during a solar eclipse, even with sunglasses, as it can seriously damage our eyes; special safe methods should be used to view it.

topics.meaning: A lunar eclipse happens when the Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon, casting its shadow on the Moon.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A lunar eclipse occurs when the Earth comes exactly between the Sun and the Moon, so that the Earth's shadow falls on the Moon and the Moon is hidden, fully or partly. During a lunar eclipse, the bright full Moon becomes dark or reddish for some time, until the Moon moves out of the Earth's shadow. A lunar eclipse always happens on a full Moon day, because that is when the Earth is between the Sun and the Moon. Unlike a solar eclipse, a lunar eclipse is completely safe to watch with the naked eye. Eclipses are natural events caused by the straight-line travel of light and the movement of the Earth and Moon.

topics.topic: Importance of the Sun, Earth and Moon
topics.meaning: The Sun, Earth and Moon are deeply connected and their movements give us light, heat, day, night, months and seasons.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Sun, the Earth and the Moon are closely connected and very important for life. The Sun is the main source of light and heat for the Earth; without it, there would be no life, no plants making food, and no warmth. The Earth's rotation gives us day and night, and its revolution with a tilted axis gives us the seasons. The Moon, going around the Earth, gives us the phases that have long been used to make calendars and mark months and festivals. The movements of these three bodies also cause eclipses. Understanding how the Sun, Earth and Moon move and affect one another helps us understand time, seasons and many events in the sky.
